a:r.a:t: This slide shows the three-layer runtime and it is worth pausing on why the system is architected this way rather than as a single monolith. The three layers are RuntimeService, RuntimeKernel, and QueryLoop, and each exists for a specific architectural reason. RuntimeService is the outermost layer. It owns session lifecycle, routing, authorization, and post-turn memory maintenance. It does not know HOW to run an agent -- only WHICH agent to run. This separation means you can change routing logic, add new auth policies, or modify session management without touching agent execution code at all. It also means the routing layer can be tested independently with mock agents. RuntimeKernel is the middle layer. It handles agent context setup: resolving effective capabilities, building the ToolContext with all runtime dependencies, instantiating tools based on the agent definition, and preparing LLM providers. Critically, the Kernel is where the coordinator-vs-direct-execution decision happens. If agent.mode is "coordinator," the Kernel delegates to KernelCoordinatorController rather than QueryLoop. The Kernel also manages session scoping for parallel workers -- each worker gets an isolated copy of the parent SessionState via _build_scoped_worker_state(). This is essential for safe parallelism: without session isolation, two workers writing to the same session would create race conditions and corrupt state. QueryLoop is the innermost layer -- the actual execution engine. It implements the ReAct loop, handles all 8 execution modes (basic, react, rag, planner, finalizer, verifier, memory_maintainer, coordinator), manages context budgeting to fit within token limits, and handles tool execution within the reasoning loop. By keeping QueryLoop focused purely on execution, you gain three benefits. First, testability: you can test the ReAct loop with mock tools and providers, without needing real sessions or routers. Second, reusability: every worker spawned by the coordinator gets its own QueryLoop instance, running in its own thread with its own step limits. The same execution code serves both top-level requests and coordinator subtasks. Third, independent evolution: you can add a new execution mode -- say a "reviewer" mode -- by modifying only QueryLoop, without touching the Kernel or Service. The try/finally pattern around progress cleanup prevents resource leaks. The circuit breaker chain means if an agent's provider is down, the system falls back to basic mode with a different provider. If even that fails, it returns a graceful degraded message. API callers can pass requested_agent_override to force a specific agent, bypassing the router entirely.
